title: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine

items.subtopic: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine
items.title: Why Physical Examination Remains Crucial Despite Technological Advances
items.content: Despite advancements in medical technology and AI, the physical examination retains its importance due to its role in fostering patient-physician relationships. Patients value the personal interaction that comes with a thorough physical exam.
keyPoints: Physical exams are essential for maintaining patient trust and satisfaction.
Patients often express dissatisfaction when they feel neglected during physical examinations.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine

items.subtopic: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine
items.title: How Healthcare System Changes Impact Patient-Physician Interactions
items.content: The merging of healthcare systems and decline of individual practices have not diminished the need for personal patient care. Patients continue to seek a caring and attentive physician, emphasizing the importance of physical examinations.
keyPoints: Healthcare system changes have led to more impersonal interactions.
Patients judge the quality of their care based on personal attention during exams.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)
items.title: The Revolutionary Impact of Percussion
items.content: Leopold Auenbrugger's introduction of percussion in the early 18th century marked a significant advancement in physical diagnosis, allowing physicians to 'look' into the living body by detecting changes in sound when tapping on the chest.
keyPoints: Auenbrugger published his findings in 1761, revolutionizing internal examination.
Percussion enabled detection of cardiomegaly and pleural effusions.
Skodaic resonance and Kronig's isthmus are notable additions to percussion techniques.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)
items.title: The Role of Bedside Instruments in Clinical Diagnosis
items.content: The development and adoption of various bedside instruments, such as the stethoscope, ophthalmoscope, clinical thermometer, reflex hammer, and blood pressure cuff, significantly enhanced diagnostic capabilities from the 18th to the 19th centuries.
keyPoints: Laënnec invented the stethoscope in 1816.
Helmholtz introduced the ophthalmoscope in 1850.
Wunderlich developed the clinical thermometer in 1868.
Erb and Westphal described the reflex hammer in 1875.
Riva-Rocci created the blood pressure cuff in 1896.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)
items.title: Joseph Bell's Mastery of Clinical Examination
items.content: Professor Joseph Bell exemplified clinical examination excellence by using keen observation and inference to diagnose patients, influencing Arthur Conan Doyle's creation of Sherlock Holmes.
keyPoints: Bell used inspection before palpation, percussion, and auscultation.
Identified patient details through accent, clay on shoes, coat size, and dermatitis.
Inferred patient's origin, route, and occupation from subtle clues.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.title: Why the Physical Examination Remains Gold Standard for Certain Diagnoses
items.content: For certain medical disorders, such as Parkinson's disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, there are no definitive laboratory or imaging studies available. In these cases, bedside physical examination findings serve as the gold standard for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Physical examination is crucial when no lab/imaging tests can diagnose a condition.
Examples include Parkinson's disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.title: How Evidence-Based Physical Examination Complements Imaging and Lab Tests
items.content: Evidence-based physical examination maneuvers can refine the probability of a disease diagnosis, guiding further imaging or laboratory testing. This approach emphasizes clinical judgment over reliance solely on external data.
keyPoints: Physical exam refines disease probability pre-imaging/labs.
Emphasizes clinical evidence base as per Alvan Feinstein's philosophy.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.title: The Role of Physical Examination in Uncovering Asymptomatic Diseases
items.content: A thorough physical examination can reveal clues to other asymptomatic or unrelated disease states, which may be treatable and could otherwise go unnoticed.
keyPoints: Physical exam can uncover asymptomatic diseases.
Helps identify potentially treatable conditions.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.title: Interobserver Agreement in Clinical Observations
items.content: The reliability of clinical observations, including physical examinations and imaging interpretations, is quantified using the kappa statistic ($ \kappa $), which measures interobserver agreement. This value ranges from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating better agreement.
keyPoints: $ \kappa $ ranges from 0 (agreement by chance) to 1 (perfect agreement).
Fair agreement: $ \kappa $ of 0.2–0.4.
Moderate agreement: $ \kappa $ of 0.4–0.6.
Substantial agreement: $ \kappa $ of 0.6–0.8.
Example: Chest radiograph infiltrate recognition has a $ \kappa $ of 0.38–0.58 (fair to moderate).
Example: Interstitial edema on chest radiograph has a $ \kappa $ of 0.83 (better agreement).
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.title: Diagnostic Accuracy: Pretest Probability and Likelihood Ratios
items.content: Clinicians use pretest probabilities, likelihood ratios (LRs), and posttest probabilities to refine diagnostic accuracy. LRs help estimate the impact of clinical findings on disease probability.
keyPoints: Pretest probability for pneumonia with cough and fever: 15–35%.
Pretest probability for hepatopulmonary syndrome in cirrhosis: 14–37%.
Specificity of clubbing for HPS: 64–96%; Sensitivity: 12–91%.
LRs >1 increase posttest probability; LRs <1 decrease it.
Rule of thumb for LRs: 2, 5, and 10 increase disease probability by 15%, 30%, and 45%; 0.5, 0.2, and 0.1 reduce it by the same percentages.
Example: Clubbing in cirrhosis with LR of 4.3 raises HPS probability from 30% to about 55–60%.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.title: Combining Independent Clinical Findings
items.content: When multiple independent clinical findings are present, their likelihood ratios can be combined to refine the posttest probability of a diagnosis.
keyPoints: Independent findings with different pathogenesis can be combined.
Example: Clubbing (LR 4.3) and cyanosis (LR 4.4) in cirrhosis.
Combining these LRs raises HPS posttest probability to about 80–90%.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.title: Utilizing Likelihood Ratios to Enhance Diagnostic Accuracy
items.content: Likelihood ratios (LRs) help clinicians focus on physical findings that significantly alter the probability of a diagnosis. By concentrating on LRs greater than or equal to 3.0 for increased probability and less than or equal to 0.3 for decreased probability, clinicians can efficiently identify key diagnostic indicators while disregarding those with minimal impact.
keyPoints: LRs improve accuracy by focusing on significant findings (≥3.0 increase, ≤0.3 decrease).
Example: In ascites diagnosis, fluid wave (LR = 5) and edema (LR = 3.8) increase probability; absence of edema (LR = 0.2) decreases it.
Disregard findings with LRs between 0.3 and 3.0 as they minimally affect probability.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.title: Physical Signs as Decision Points in Patient Management
items.content: Mastering specific physical diagnosis maneuvers and 'stop rules' allows clinicians to distinguish serious conditions from benign ones, potentially ending the workup early. For instance, using the HINTS battery in patients with acute vertigo can significantly reduce the likelihood of a posterior circulation stroke.
keyPoints: HINTS battery (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Skew Deviation Test) used for dizziness evaluation.
Positive head impulse test, absence of direction-changing nystagmus, and absence of skew deviation decrease probability of posterior circulation stroke (LR = 0.02).
Stop rules can safely end workup if conditions are met.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.title: Identifying Unsuspected and Asymptomatic Disorders Through Physical Examination
items.content: An attentive physical examination can reveal clues to asymptomatic or unsuspected conditions, allowing for early intervention. Observations such as diffuse thyroid enlargement, xanthelasma, acanthosis nigricans, coxalgic gait, Trendelenburg gait, and circumduction gait provide insights into potential underlying disorders.
keyPoints: Observing physical signs can reveal asymptomatic conditions (e.g., goiter, hypercholesterolemia).
Coxalgic gait indicates painful hip; Trendelenburg gait suggests gluteus medius weakness.
Circumduction gait may indicate past hemiplegia.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.title: Physical Examination Identifies Critical Conditions Overlooked by Imaging
items.content: The physical examination can reveal critical conditions that may be missed or misinterpreted in imaging studies, such as distinguishing between compressive and ischemic causes of third cranial nerve palsy based on pupillary response.
keyPoints: A dilated pupil suggests a compressive etiology like an aneurysm; a normal pupil suggests ischemia.
Case study: Patient with suspected cavernous meningioma had a spared pupil, suggesting ischemia; full recovery confirmed this.
Another patient had an abnormal pupil but initially normal CT angiogram; further review revealed a posterior communicating aneurysm.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.title: Physical Examination Prevents Misdiagnosis and Unnecessary Interventions
items.content: A thorough physical examination can prevent misdiagnoses that lead to unnecessary interventions, such as hospitalization for conditions initially labeled as 'cellulitis' but later identified as eczema or lymphedema.
keyPoints: Routing patients with suspected 'cellulitis' through a dermatology clinic reveals alternative diagnoses in about one-third of cases.
Avoids unnecessary hospitalization and antibiotic use by identifying conditions like eczema or lymphedema.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.title: Physical Examination as a Ritual Enhances Patient-Physician Relationship
items.content: The physical examination serves as a meaningful ritual that strengthens the patient-physician relationship, fostering trust and ensuring comprehensive care.
keyPoints: Patients reveal sensitive information during exams not shared with others.
Examination involves unique interactions not typical in society, emphasizing its special nature.
Physicians have fiduciary responsibility to conduct thorough examinations as part of patient care.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.title: The Role of Physical Examination in Patient Trust and Diagnosis
items.content: The physical examination serves as a ritual that transitions patients from self-sufficiency to seeking help, establishing trust with the physician. It validates symptoms by localizing them on the patient's body rather than relying solely on distant radiologic reports or lab tests.
keyPoints: Physical examination signals transition and trust
Validates presenting symptoms through localization
Prevents dehumanization compared to imaging/labs
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Importance of Skillful Physical Examination in Medical Training
items.content: Bedside skills are crucial and best learned through apprenticeship, where trainees observe and practice under the guidance of experienced role models. The decline of bedside rounds hampers this essential skill transfer.
keyPoints: Skills acquired through apprenticeship
Role models demonstrate and provide feedback
Erosion of bedside rounds is detrimental
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Placebo and Nocebo Effects in Physical Examination
items.content: A well-executed physical examination can have a positive placebo effect, enhancing patient outcomes through the ritual's context and execution. Conversely, a poor examination may induce a nocebo effect, negatively impacting patient interactions.
keyPoints: Positive effects from skilled examinations (placebo)
Negative effects from clumsy examinations (nocebo)
Context and execution influence psychobiological outcomes
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION

items.question: What is one potential consequence of missing critical findings during a physical examination?
options: It can lead to unnecessary dietary restrictions.

items.explanation: Missing critical findings can delay diagnosis and treatment, leading to potential harm.

items.question: Which of the following best describes why physical examination is considered safe?
options: It carries no added cost and uses minimal equipment.
It always leads to immediate diagnosis.
items.explanation: The physical examination is safe because it has no added cost, is immediately accessible, and uses no advanced technology beyond basic instruments.

items.question: What range of the kappa statistic ($\kappa$) indicates substantial agreement in interobserver reliability?

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.question: What is the kappa value for interobserver agreement in recognizing interstitial edema on a chest radiograph?
items.explanation: The kappa value for recognizing interstitial edema is 0.83, indicating good agreement.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.question: Which physical examination maneuver has a kappa statistic ($\kappa$) of nearly perfect agreement?
items.explanation: The elicitation of a positive abdominalogular test has a kappa statistic ($\kappa$) of 0.92, indicating nearly perfect agreement.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.question: What is the likelihood ratio (LR) for finger clubbing in detecting hepatopulmonary syndrome (HPS) in a patient with cirrhosis?
items.explanation: The likelihood ratio for finger clubbing in detecting HPS is 4.3.

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following is a reason why likelihood ratios (LRs) are useful in physical examination?
options: They allow clinicians to focus on findings with LRs close to 1.
They help identify findings that minimally change probability.
They enable quick identification of findings that significantly alter diagnosis probability.
They eliminate the need for further diagnostic testing.
items.explanation: LRs allow clinicians to quickly focus on findings with large values (3.0 or more) and those close to zero (0.3 or less), which significantly alter diagnosis probability.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.question: In the context of evaluating a patient with suspected ascites, which finding is NOT considered to decrease the probability?
items.explanation: The presence of a fluid wave increases the probability of ascites, whereas absence of edema and absence of flank dullness decrease it.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.question: What is an example of a 'stop rule' in physical examination?
options: The HINTS battery for dizziness evaluation

items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.question: Which gait abnormality suggests past hemiplegia?
items.explanation: Circumduction of the foot, along with reduced arm swing on the same side, suggests past hemiplegia.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.question: Which condition is suggested by clubbing and Horner's syndrome in the context of pulmonary hypertrophic osteoarthropathy?
items.explanation: Clubbing and Horner's syndrome suggest pulmonary hypertrophic osteoarthropathy caused by a malignancy in the lung.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.question: What is the significance of 'dew drops on rose petals' rash observed during cardiac catheterization?
items.explanation: 'Dew drops on rose petals' rash is indicative of herpes zoster involving a dermatome.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.statement: Physical examination findings are often considered less reliable than imaging or laboratory tests.
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes the importance of physical examination and its role as a gold standard in certain diagnoses.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.statement: The physical examination can uncover asymptomatic or unrelated disease states.
items.explanation: Physical examination frequently uncovers clues to other disease states that are asymptomatic or unrelated to the patient's presenting complaint.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.statement: Interobserver agreement for the classification of coronary artery lesions on angiogram is substantial.
items.explanation: The interobserver agreement for classifying coronary artery lesions on angiogram is only fair, with a kappa value of 0.33.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.statement: The likelihood ratio (LR) for finger clubbing in detecting HPS increases the probability by about 25–30%.
items.explanation: An LR of 4.3 for finger clubbing raises the probability of HPS by approximately 25–30%.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.statement: The HINTS battery can increase the probability of a posterior circulation stroke.
items.explanation: A positive HINTS battery markedly decreases the probability of a posterior circulation stroke.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.statement: An incomplete physical examination can lead to medical errors.
items.explanation: Failure to do or an incomplete examination can result in diagnostic delay and inappropriate treatment.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.statement: A careful physical examination can sometimes challenge radiologic interpretations.
items.explanation: Physical examinations may raise doubt on radiologic interpretations, as seen in cases involving cranial nerve palsy.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.statement: Errors in physical examination are often due to the electronic medical record system.
items.explanation: Oversights in physical examinations are usually due to cursory exams or lack of continuity, not directly because of electronic records.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.statement: Patients can recognize when a physical examination is done well and with pride.
items.explanation: Patients are expert judges of the skills of professionals, including physicians.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.statement: The physical examination is no longer necessary due to advances in imaging and laboratory tests.
items.explanation: The physical examination remains critical for diagnosis and patient assessment.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)
items.assertion: The German School emphasized experimental medicine and laboratory skills.
items.reason: Johannes Müller was a key figure in the German School during the 1850s.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)
items.assertion: Arthur Conan Doyle's character Sherlock Holmes was based on Professor Joseph Bell.
items.reason: Professor Bell was known for his inferential skills in clinical examination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.assertion: The physical examination is crucial for diagnosing diseases like Parkinson's disease.
items.reason: There are no laboratory or imaging studies that make the diagnosis of Parkinson's disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the physical examination is crucial for Parkinson's disease.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)
items.assertion: The physical examination has no added cost and uses advanced technology.
items.reason: It is immediately accessible and safe for the patient.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the physical examination does not use advanced technology, but it is true that it is immediately accessible and safe.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · HISTORICAL EVOLUTION OF THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2) | FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 1)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.assertion: The interobserver agreement for recognizing the presence of an infiltrate on chest radiograph is substantial.
items.reason: The kappa value ($\kappa$) for this recognition ranges from 0.38 to 0.58.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the agreement is only fair to moderate, not substantial.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)
items.assertion: Combining independent findings can increase posttest probability of a diagnosis.
items.reason: Each finding contributes independently to the overall likelihood ratio, adjusting pretest probability accordingly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why combining independent findings increases posttest probability.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 2)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.assertion: The Trendelenburg gait indicates weakness of the hip abductor.
items.reason: It is characterized by a dramatic lateral lean over the hip when bearing weight.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)
items.assertion: Observing xanthelasma can lead to early intervention for hypercholesterolemia.
items.reason: Xanthelasma is a clue to diseases that patients may be unaware of but are consequential.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 3)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.assertion: The physical examination is a ritual meaningful to both patient and physician.
items.reason: It involves unique interactions and examinations that are not typical in society, highlighting its significance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the physical exam is considered a ritual.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)
items.assertion: Rapid patient turnover leads to more accurate diagnoses.
items.reason: It allows for frequent handoffs and continuity of care by multiple teams.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; rapid patient turnover actually contributes to diagnostic errors due to lack of continuity.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 4)

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.assertion: A well-executed physical examination preserves the patient's sense of identity.
items.reason: It validates symptoms by localizing them on their body rather than relying solely on distant reports or tests.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why a well-executed physical examination preserves the patient's sense of identity.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
items.assertion: Bedside skills can be effectively taught through conference room discussions.
items.reason: This setting allows for the discussion of patient data on computers.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; bedside skills are best passed on by role models in a hands-on environment, not through conference room discussions.
items.reference: The Value of the Physical Examination in Modern Medicine · FIVE REASONS THE PHYSICAL EXAMINATION REMAINS VERY RELEVANT (part 5) | CONCLUSION
